system_prompt:
You are distilling one chapter of a guitar method book into a short summary.

The user will give you a chapter file with topic-organized verbatim quotes
plus the human-rationale notes for each. Write a one-paragraph summary (4-7
sentences) that captures:

  - what this chapter teaches (the techniques, concepts, rules, examples)
  - the master's characteristic moves visible in this chapter (if any)
  - the load-bearing terminology this chapter introduces

Reference the topic labels from the chapter file when useful. Do not invent
information not present in the chapter. If the chapter is biographical or
introductory rather than instructional, say so explicitly.

Output just the paragraph — no preamble, no JSON, no markdown formatting.
user_prompt:
Chapter 2: Melody Chord Progressions, Introductions, Arpeggio and String Bass Styles (Lessons 6-8)

<<<CHAPTER FILE
# Chapter 2 — Melody Chord Progressions, Introductions, Arpeggio and String Bass Styles (Lessons 6-8)

_Pages 6-9._

> **Verbatim-quote constraint.** The quotes below are exact substrings of the raw transcript produced by `pdftotext`. The transcript may contain extraction artifacts where the book's typeface confuses the tool — e.g. `7irst` or `<irst` for `first`, `speci7ic` for `specific`. **Do NOT manually fix these in this file**: the quote-fidelity validator compares against the raw transcript, so a 'fixed' quote will fail verification and break Stage 2 idempotency. If a future pipeline iteration adds artifact-normalization, it must normalize the transcript and these files together.

## Transpose Lesson 4 by hand

> In the keys of F, Ao and Bé major, simply take three pieces of manuscript paper, and transpose Lesson No. 4 to
> each key. After you have done this and you are sure that they are right, practice each example until you know
> them all by heart in each key.

**Significance (p. 6).** Operationalises the transposition mandate as a concrete manuscript-paper exercise rather than a mental abstraction.

## Melody chord progressions

> Now for melody chord progressions.
> We are still working in the keys of F, G, A’ and Bé. We will work with the keys of C, Dé and Ee later.

**Significance (p. 6).** Marks the pivot from rhythm comping to melody-chord playing while preserving the four-key training scope.

## Chromatic intro practice

> Let’s work on simple introductions first. Try these examples in G major. You may find some of these very hard to
> play, but if you practice them chromatically up and down the finger board you will soon become accustomed to them.

**Significance (p. 6).** Reapplies the chromatic-up-the-neck drill specifically to intros, treating physical familiarity with the shape as the path to fluency.

## Most important melody chords

> They are your most important melody chords for these four keys. So give them all the attention you possibly can.

**Significance (p. 8).** Designates the Lesson 6 intro shapes as the canonical melody-chord vocabulary for F, G, A♭ and B♭.

## Vary rhythm to create intros

> Another way to get new ideas is to change the rhythm of the introduction. Try playing them in arpeggio style,
> or try playing the bottom note of the chord first, then the chord.

**Significance (p. 8).** Introduces the rhythmic-displacement trick (root-then-chord, arpeggiation) as Baker's go-to method for generating new intros from a fixed chord set.

## Combine intros creatively

> Another thing you must do, is to try making other intro’s by changing these around in as many ways as you can.
> This may not be so easy at first, but if you devote enough time to it, you will find yourself making all kinds of
> intro’s from these few chords.

**Significance (p. 8).** Authorises combinatorial recomposition of a small chord set as the engine of unlimited intro-writing.

## Arpeggio and string bass styles

> In this Lesson I have a few ideas on how to use the Arpeggio, and the string bass.

**Significance (p. 8).** Names the two characteristic accompaniment textures (arpeggio, walking string-bass) that the book elevates above plain strumming.

## Reworking Intro No. 2

> Using Introduction No. 2 in Lesson No. 6 work them out and try to make up new ones.

**Significance (p. 8).** Pins the arpeggio/string-bass studies to a specific intro template, giving the student a concrete object on which to apply the texture variations.

CHAPTER FILE>>>
